title: Bound the bit-length of the largest intermediate exponent (blow-up)

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
This is the crux of the problem. During collection in an **infinite** polycyclic group,
iterated conjugation multiplies exponents, so the integers stored in the exponent vector can
grow far beyond the input exponents. The whole complexity answer is dominated by a bound on
the bit-length $B$ of the largest exponent appearing at any point.

**What must be shown.** An explicit bound $B=B(\ell,n,b)$, where $b$ is the input/relation
exponent bit-length, valid for all inputs.

**Why this is hard and unavoidable.** Consider $G=\langle g_1,g_2\mid g_2^{g_1}=g_2^{2}\rangle$
($=\mathbb Z\rtimes\mathbb Z$). For the input $g_1^{-\ell}\,g_2\,g_1^{\ell}$ one has
$g_1^{-\ell} g_2 g_1^{\ell}=g_2^{2^{\ell}}$, an exponent of value $2^\ell$, i.e. bit-length
$\Theta(\ell)$ — with $n=2$ and $b=O(1)$ fixed (verified numerically). Hence **any correct
complexity statement must include an exponent term that is $\Theta(\ell)$ in bit-length**, and
a claim of "polynomial in $\ell,n,b$ bit operations with constant exponent size" is false.

**Deliverable.** Track the conjugation action on the torsion-free abelian sections as
multiplication by bounded-entry integer matrices; let $K$ bound the absolute exponents in the
relation RHSs. Prove a bound of the form $B \le O(\ell\log K)+b+\dots$ (exact form to be
determined and justified by an induction down the polycyclic series), and prove it is tight via
the family above. Treat the finite case ($\max r_i=R<\infty$) separately, where $B=O(\log R)$
a priori.
